---
title: "stalen alternatieve substraten"
author: "Jiayue"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
  html_document:
    toc: true
---
```{r}
library("readxl")
library("dplyr")
library("tidyr")
library("ggplot2")
library("magrittr")
library("lattice")
library("car")


data_all <- read_xlsx("D:/0_personal_laptop_Jiayue/0_PhD_study/SUSANA_biomass/data_analysis/stalen alternatieve substraten.xlsx")
data<- data_all[c(1:82),]  

# Convert several columns to factors (categorical variables).
data <- data %>%
  mutate(
    ab        = factor(ab),
    location  = factor(location),
    substrate = factor(substrate),
    in_out    = factor(in_out)
  )


str(data)

```

```{r}
# Plot simple histograms to inspect marginal distributions.
hist(data$TN,  col = "white",  main = "TN",  xlab = "TN")
hist(data$TOC, col = "white",   main = "TOC", xlab = "TOC")
hist(data$d0.5,col = "white", main = "d0.5", xlab = "d0.5")

# Arrange the plotting area as 1 row × 3 columns.
# The following boxplots compare responses across 'substrate'.
cols <- c("pink", "lightblue")
par(mfrow=c(1,3))
plot(data$substrate, data$TN,  main="TN",  ylab="TN",  xlab="substrate", col=cols)
plot(data$substrate, data$TOC, main="TOC", ylab="TOC", xlab="substrate", col=cols)
plot(data$substrate, data$d0.5, main="d0.5", ylab="d0.5", xlab="substrate", col=cols)

# Same layout, but now comparing across 'in_out'.
cols <- c("pink", "lightblue")
par(mfrow=c(1,3))
plot(data$in_out, data$TN,   main="TN",   ylab="TN",   xlab="in/out", col=cols)
plot(data$in_out, data$TOC,  main="TOC",  ylab="TOC",  xlab="in/out", col=cols)
plot(data$in_out, data$d0.5, main="d0.5", ylab="d0.5", xlab="in/out", col=cols)

# Lattice boxplots: response ~ substrate, faceted (|) by in_out.
trellis.par.set(list(box.rectangle = list(col = "black"),
                     box.umbrella  = list(col = "black"),
                     box.dot       = list(col = "black"),
                     plot.symbol   = list(col = "black")))
bwplot(TN ~ substrate | in_out, data=data, main="TN",   ylab="TN")
bwplot(TOC ~ substrate | in_out, data=data, main="TOC",  ylab="TOC")
bwplot(d0.5 ~ substrate | in_out, data=data, main="d0.5", ylab="d0.5")


# Clear differences were found between the alternative and control substrates. The alternative substrate contained considerably higher total nitrogen (TN) and total organic carbon (TOC) but had a smaller median grain size (d0.5) compared with the control. In contrast, sampling position (inside vs. outside) showed no clear effect on any of the measured parameters. 
```

```{r}
ggplot(data, aes(x=in_out, y=TN)) + 
  geom_boxplot(aes(fill=substrate)) +
  ggtitle("TN") + labs(y="%TN", x="substrate") +
    theme(legend.position="none") +
   scale_fill_manual(values=c("pink", "lightblue")) +
  facet_grid(. ~ substrate)

ggplot(data, aes(x=in_out, y=TOC)) + 
  geom_boxplot(aes(fill=substrate)) +
  ggtitle("TOC") + labs(y="%TOC", x="substrate") +
  theme(legend.position="none") +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("pink", "lightblue")) +
  facet_grid(. ~ substrate)

ggplot(data, aes(x=in_out, y=d0.5)) + 
  geom_boxplot(aes(fill=substrate)) +
  ggtitle("d0.5") + labs(y="d0.5", x="substrate") +
    theme(legend.position="none") +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("pink", "lightblue")) +
  facet_grid(. ~ substrate)

# Boxplots illustrated this pattern, with consistently higher TN and TOC values in the alternative substrate, while d0.5 was greater in the control.
```

```{r}
# Base-R boxplots again with custom colors (vector recycled over groups).
par(mfrow=c(1,3))
plot(data$substrate, data$TN,   main="TN",  ylab="%TN",   xlab="substrate", col=c("pink","lightblue"))
plot(data$substrate, data$TOC,  main="TOC", ylab="%TOC",  xlab="substrate", col=c("pink","lightblue"))
plot(data$substrate, data$d0.5, main="d0.5", ylab="d0.5", xlab="substrate", col=c("pink","lightblue"))

par(mfrow=c(1,3))
plot(data$in_out, data$TN,   main="TN",  ylab="%TN",   xlab="in/out", col=c("pink","lightblue"))
plot(data$in_out, data$TOC,  main="TOC", ylab="%TOC",  xlab="in/out", col=c("pink","lightblue"))
plot(data$in_out, data$d0.5, main="d0.5", ylab="d0.5", xlab="in/out", col=c("pink","lightblue"))

```

```{r}
# base-mean-var & assumptions
# Prepare a 1×3 panel to examine mean–variance relationships per group.
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))

# Compute groupwise means and variances of TN by the interaction of substrate and in_out.
m_TN <- with(data, tapply(TN,  interaction(substrate, in_out, drop = TRUE), mean, na.rm = TRUE))
v_TN <- with(data, tapply(TN,  interaction(substrate, in_out, drop = TRUE), var,  na.rm = TRUE))
plot(v_TN ~ m_TN)

m_TOC <- with(data, tapply(TOC, interaction(substrate, in_out, drop = TRUE), mean, na.rm = TRUE))
v_TOC <- with(data, tapply(TOC, interaction(substrate, in_out, drop = TRUE), var,  na.rm = TRUE))
plot(v_TOC ~ m_TOC)

m_d0.5 <- with(data, tapply(d0.5, interaction(substrate, in_out, drop = TRUE), mean, na.rm = TRUE))
v_d0.5 <- with(data, tapply(d0.5, interaction(substrate, in_out, drop = TRUE), var,  na.rm = TRUE))
plot(v_d0.5 ~ m_d0.5)

# Shapiro–Wilk normality test within each interaction group for each variable.
with(data, tapply(TN,   interaction(substrate, in_out, drop = TRUE), function(x) shapiro.test(x)))
with(data, tapply(TOC,  interaction(substrate, in_out, drop = TRUE), function(x) shapiro.test(x)))
with(data, tapply(d0.5, interaction(substrate, in_out, drop = TRUE), function(x) shapiro.test(x)))

# Levene’s test for homogeneity of variances across the interaction groups.
leveneTest(data$TN,   interaction(data$substrate, data$in_out, drop = TRUE))
leveneTest(data$TOC,  interaction(data$substrate, data$in_out, drop = TRUE))
leveneTest(data$d0.5, interaction(data$substrate, data$in_out, drop = TRUE))

# Normality tests (Shapiro–Wilk) showed that several groups deviated significantly from a normal distribution (p < 0.05), whereas Levene’s tests indicated no significant differences in variance among groups (p > 0.05). Because the normality assumption was violated but variances were homogeneous, non-parametric Wilcoxon rank-sum tests were applied for group comparisons.
```


```{r}
# Wilcoxon rank–sum (Mann–Whitney) tests comparing distributions between two groups.
wilcox.test(TN   ~ substrate, data)  # significant
wilcox.test(TOC  ~ substrate, data)  # highly significant
wilcox.test(d0.5 ~ substrate, data)  # significant

wilcox.test(TN   ~ in_out, data)     # not significant
wilcox.test(TOC  ~ in_out, data)     # not significant
wilcox.test(d0.5 ~ in_out, data)     # not significant

# These tests confirmed that TN, TOC, and d0.5 all differed significantly between substrate types, but none of the variables showed a significant difference between the inside and outside sampling positions.
```
